coredata.dc:title: The synthesis of high bright silver nanoclusters with aggregation-induced emission for detection of tetracycline

coredata.dc:description: In this study, the silver nanoclusters (Ag NCs) with aggregation-induced emission property were synthesized successfully by reflux method using silver nitrate (AgNO3) as raw material and N-acetyl-L-cysteine (NAC) as the reductant and capped ligand. The synthesis process was simple, environmentally friendly and the process did not require the addition of other surfactants or common reductant such as sodium borohydride, ascorbic acid. The obtained Ag NCs possessed the special Ag(0)@Ag(I)–SR core-shell structure with aggregation-induced emission characteristic. The Ag NCs exhibited strong green luminescence with an optimal emission wavelength of 494 nm, high quantum yield of 0.44, microsecond long life of 11.07 μs and high content of Ag(I) component. In addition, the obtained Ag NCs were used to detect the tetracycline (TC) with the range of linearity from 1.12 μM to 230 μM and a detection limit of 0.47 μM on account of the quenching mechanism of the internal filtration effect (IFE) and the static quenching effect between tetracycline and Ag NCs. The proposed sensing method based on NAC@Ag NCs showed good sensitivity and selectivity for tetracycline, wide range of linearity, low detection limit and strong anti-interference ability. This method had been applied to detect tetracycline concentration reliably and accurately in actual sample milk.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of the synthesis of Ag(0)@Ag(I)−NAC@Ag NCs (NAC@Ag NCs) with aggregation-induced emission.

Fluorescence emission spectra of NAC@Ag NCs synthesized under different molar ratios of NAC/AgNO3 (A), different reaction temperatures (B) and different reaction times (C). Inset: the relationship between the fluorescence intensity and different reaction conditions.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of NAC@Ag NCs (black line), AgNO3 (grey line) and NAC (orange line), normalized fluorescence excitation (red line) and emission (blue line) spectra of Ag NCs. Inset: photographs of the Ag NCs under visible light (left) and UV light (right) irradiation at 365 nm. (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of Ag NCs under the different excitation wavelengths from 300 nm to 400 nm. (C) Fluorescence decay curves of Ag NCs. (D) TEM image of the as-prepared Ag NCs. Inset: The particle size histogram by calculating the size of a hundred individual particles. (E) TEM image under high magnification of the Ag NCs. (F) FT-IR spectra of pure NAC (black line) and NAC@Ag NCs (red line).
(A) XPS full-scan spectrum of the as-prepared NAC@Ag NCs. (B) XPS spectra of Ag 3d peak for the luminescent NAC@Ag NCs.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of NAC@Ag NCs in different pH values of BR buffer solutions. (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of the AgNCs against different NaCl concentrations. (C) Fluorescence emission spectra of the AgNCs against different BR buffer concentrations. The insets in A, B and C show the corresponding maximum emission against different pH, different NaCl concentrations and different BR buffer concentrations, respectively. (D) Plots of the fluorescence intensity of Ag NCs in in the mixture of BR buffer solution and ethanol (v:v = 1:1) (a) and BR buffer solution (b) versus the time of UV irradiation. (E) Effect of the amount of ethanol on the fluorescence intensity of Ag NCs. (F) Fluorescence emission spectra of Ag NCs under different storage time. Inset: photographs of the Ag NCs under UV light irradiation (365 nm) at one-month storage intervals.

(A) Effect of the amount of NAC@Ag NCs on fluorescence intensity without and with TC, where F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity in the absence TC and presence of TC. (B) Time-dependent fluorescence intensity of NAC@Ag NCs upon addition of TC. The concentration of TC is 100 μM. (C) Fluorescence emission spectra of NAC@Ag NCs upon addition of various concentrations of TC in the mixed solution of 500 μl BR buffer solution and 500 μl ethanol. (D) The relationship between the relative fluorescence intensity log (F0/F) and the concentration of TC in the range of 1.12 μM – 230 μM, where F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity in the absence of TC and presence of different concentrations of TC, respectively.

(A) and (B) The relative fluorescence intensity values [(F0–F)/F0] of NAC@Ag NCs with different small molecules and ions. The concentrations of TC and other interfering analytes were 0.00018 M and 0.0018 M. Red and green bars represented (F0–F)/F0 of Ag NCs before and after addition of TC with coexistence of various interfering analytes. (C) Photographs of NAC@Ag NCs with different small molecules and ions solution viewed under UV light at 365 nm.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectrum of TC (red line), and the fluorescence excitation spectrum of Ag NCs (black line). (B) FT-IR spectra of Ag NCs without (red line) and with TC (black line). (C) UV–vis absorption spectra of Ag NCs without (black line) and with TC (red line). (D) Histogram of zeta potential of Ag NCs in the absence and presence of TC.
Comparison of the proposed method with other methods for the detection of TC based on fluorescence nanomaterials.
The results of recovery studies of TC in milk samples using the proposed sensing method based on NAC@Ag NCs and HPLC method.

head.ce:title: The synthesis of high bright silver nanoclusters with aggregation-induced emission for detection of tetracycline

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, the silver nanoclusters (Ag NCs) with aggregation-induced emission property were synthesized successfully by reflux method using silver nitrate (AgNO3) as raw material and N-acetyl-L-cysteine (NAC) as the reductant and capped ligand. The synthesis process was simple, environmentally friendly and the process did not require the addition of other surfactants or common reductant such as sodium borohydride, ascorbic acid. The obtained Ag NCs possessed the special Ag(0)@Ag(I)–SR core-shell structure with aggregation-induced emission characteristic. The Ag NCs exhibited strong green luminescence with an optimal emission wavelength of 494 nm, high quantum yield of 0.44, microsecond long life of 11.07 μs and high content of Ag(I) component. In addition, the obtained Ag NCs were used to detect the tetracycline (TC) with the range of linearity from 1.12 μM to 230 μM and a detection limit of 0.47 μM on account of the quenching mechanism of the internal filtration effect (IFE) and the static quenching effect between tetracycline and Ag NCs. The proposed sensing method based on NAC@Ag NCs showed good sensitivity and selectivity for tetracycline, wide range of linearity, low detection limit and strong anti-interference ability. This method had been applied to detect tetracycline concentration reliably and accurately in actual sample milk.

ce:section.ce:para: Tetracycline (TC) is a broad-spectrum and effective antibiotic discovered in the mid-twentieth century [1,2]. As a common antibacterial drug, it can produce an effective inhibitory effect on the infection of gram positive and negative bacteria, rickettsia, ultravirus, spirochaeta and parasite [3]. Besides, the advantages of TC also include low manufacturing cost, small toxic, side effects, and easy oral absorption. As a result, it is used as anti-inflammatory drug to treat bacterial infections in the medical industry. TC is also used as veterinary drug to prevent, treat infections and growth agent to promote growth in the aquaculture industry [4,5]. However, the abuse of TC possesses a potential threat to human health due to bacterial resistance and antibiotic accumulation [6]. A large number of studies have shown that the overdose of TC can cause side effects and diseases such as yellow teeth, nausea and vomiting, allergic reactions, liver and kidney damage. Besides, pregnant women are more susceptible to TC hepatotoxicity [7]. Furthermore, the presence of excess TC residues in the environment inhibits the growth of microorganisms and also causes some damages to the ecological environment [8]. Therefore, in order to reduce the harm of TC to humans and the ecological environment, it is urgent to propose a valid and accurate method for the measurement of TC concentrations.

Many tetracycline detection methods have been explored, including high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [9], capillary electrophoresis (CE) [10], electrochemical method [11] and chemiluminescence (CL) [12]. Generally, HPLC detection method has high analysis cost, complex operation steps, and pre-treatment of the samples is required [13]. Moreover, the pre-treatment process usually involves a large amount of organic solvents, which leads to the disadvantages of solvent consumption and environmental pollution [14]. The separation ability of CE method is weak, and it has high requirements for pH. More importantly, the reproducibility of the analysis results is poor because the electroosmosis will vary with the composition of the sample. Electrochemical method is difficult to select appropriate receptor elements such as molecularly imprinted polymers, functional molecules, aptamer etc. and signal transducer nanomaterial, substrate, etc. [15]. CL method usually responds to a series of compounds. It is only suitable for the detection of high purity samples such as pharmaceutical preparations. In recent years, fluorescence analysis has been extensively studied in terms of analysis and detection owing to the advantages such as low cost of analysis and testing, convenient operation, good stability and sensitivity, fast signal response, real-time detection, excellent reproducibility of analysis results and almost no damage to samples. Therefore, it is reasonable to construct a high selectivity and high accuracy fluorescence analysis to detect TC.

Because of the properties of ultra-small size, good water solubility, excellent stability and biocompatibility [16–20], metal nanoclusters (NCs) have received more and more attention in many fields which include photoelectric chemiluminescence sensors, environmental analysis, cell imaging and biomedical drug detection [21–24]. The photoluminescence (PL) property of metal NCs provides a potential method to design new luminescent probes in the fields of biology, medicine and environment [25–27]. Therefore, the PL property is the most promising property among various similar molecules of metal NCs. However, compared with quantum dots (QDs), the PL quantum yield (QY) of metal NCs is still not competitive, which limits the wide application of metal NCs. This is mainly due to the facts that the interface structure and the inseparable contribution of metal cores and capped ligands are unclear, which lead to the basic principles structure and photophysical properties of the metal NCs are not fully grasped [28]. Recent studies have confirmed that the PL property of metal NCs is caused by the mixed effects of ligand-metal charge transfer (LMCT) and/or ligand-metal-metal charge transfer (LMMCT) through metal-centered triplet states [29–32]. It is worth noting that, unlike traditional QDs, metal NCs are not toxic heavy metals, which may bring about better biocompatibility in the actual environment [19,33]. Therefore, it is important to synthesize a highly luminescent metal NCs.

The Tangʼs group reported that some fluorophores emit strong light when they are in the state of aggregation or in the solid state for the first time in 2001, which was defined as aggregation-induced emission phenomenon (AIE) [34,35]. The AIE is obviously different from the traditional aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) phenomenon. Inspired by this phenomenon, some metal NCs with AIE property were also gradually synthesized [36–38]. It opens the door to solve the problem of low quantum yields of traditional metal NCs. The aggregation of NCs can inhibit the internal vibration and rotation of active molecules, whereas the active molecules are freely rotatable or vibrating in the dilute solution, thereby suppress the excited state non-radiative relaxation behavior associated with the active molecules and enhance the luminescent property of NCs [32–40]. Gold and copper nanoclusters with AIE property synthesized with thiol-containing ligands have been extensively studied in the last few years [36–38]. However, the high cost of gold limits the wide application of Au NCs. Since Cu has a higher oxidation potential (E°ox= -0.34 V) than Ag (E°ox= -0.80 V) and Au (E°ox= -1.50 V), Cu NCs are easily oxidized in aqueous media or exposed to environmental conditions [41]. Therefore, Ag NCs have become the research hotspot in the field of analytical sensing in recent years. Dendrimers, polymers, DNA and protein are generally used as protective and stable ligands for the synthesis of Ag NCs, but these Ag NCs have short emission time, low QY and ACQ phenomenon [42–45]. So the AIE exploration of Ag NCs is extremely necessary. At present, the synthesis and application of Ag NCs with AIE characteristic have only been reported in recent years. Xie et al. used thiol biomolecule tripeptide glutathione (g-Glu-Cys-Gly or GSH) to synthesize Ag NCs with AIE phenomenon in 2015 [46]. The as-prepared Ag NCs had a high QY of 0.074 and a long lifetime in microseconds. In particular, they proposed that this highly luminescent Ag NCs were associated with the especial core-shell structure of Ag(0)@Ag(I)−thiolate and wrap a large amount of Ag(I)−thiolate complexes on the Ag(0) core. Pan et al. synthesized thiosalicylic acid (TSA)-protected hydrophobic Ag NCs under nitrogen protection and self-assembled them at 0 °C to obtain aggregation-induced luminescent Ag NCs [47]. The synthesized Ag NCs had a high QY of 0.25 and a long fluorescence lifetime of 9.04 μs. In view of the fact that the fluorescence of Ag NCs was quenched by mercury ions (Hg2+), the high sensitivity and selective quantitative detection method of mercury ions was established. Ren et al. synthesized lipoic acid (LA)-protected Ag NCs with AIE property by using NaBH4 as reducing agent [48]. Aggregated Ag NCs were dispersed by introducing Cu2+. Cu2+ was easily coordinated with melamine to form a Cu2+–melamine complex ring, which made the detection of melamine possible. Zhou et al. found that the synthesized Ag NCs had AIE phenomenon in the presence of Au3+ and designed the fluorescence sensing system for the detection of biothiols [49]. However, the synthesis of Ag NCs with high QY and long luminescence lifetime is still challenging. And it is still expected that more and more controlled synthesis and potential application of Ag NCs with AIE phenomenon will be reported.

According to the previous research, thiol-containing compounds are generally selected as the capped ligands for AIE type metal nanoclusters because of the strong chemical bond between Ag and S. It is well known that the most common thiol ligands are glutathione (GSH) and cysteine (Cys). The QY of the as-synthesized Ag NCs using GSH as ligand was still low (as shown in Table S1). In the synthesis process of Cys protected Ag NCs, additional reductants were usually needed to be used and AIE property was not observed [50,51]. N-acetyl-L-cysteine (NAC) as N-acetylated derivatives of cysteine has been used in our previous work to synthesize Au NCs by one-pot hydrothermal method with high QY (0.14) [52,53], which proved NAC is an effective thiolate to prepare strongly luminescent metal nanoclusters. In this study, NAC was used as reductant and stabilizing ligand to synthesize Ag NCs by reflux method. This process did not require the addition of other surfactants or chemicals such as strong acids and bases. The reaction conditions were simple and environmentally friendly. The synthesized NAC@Ag NCs possessed an Ag(0)@Ag(I)–SR core-shell structure with AIE characteristic. It exhibited high QY of 0.44, microsecond long life of 11.07 μs and high content of Ag(I) component, which were the same as that Ag NCs exhibited AIE characteristic reported in the previous literature [54]. As displayed in Scheme 1 , the synthesized Ag NCs showed high bright green fluorescence under 365 nm ultraviolet irradiation. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity of Ag NCs decreased or even quenched clearly and instantly with the addition of TC in Ag NCs due to the internal filtration effect (IFE). Thus, a detection method for TC concentrations was established with high sensitivity and selectivity. In addition, the sensing system developed in this study was feasible to detect TC concentration in milk samples.

N-Acetyl-L-cysteine (NAC, 99 %) was purchased from International Laboratory (San Bruno, CA, USA). Silver nitrate (AgNO3, 99.9 %–100.1 %) was ordered from Shanghai Baili Biotechnology Co.,Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Absolute ethanol (CH3CH2OH) and inorganic ions (PO4 3–, CO3 2–, HCO3 –, F–, SO4 2–, Cl–, NO2 –, Ac–, SCN–, C2O4 2–, Ca2+, Mg2+, K+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Na+, Sn2+, Al3+, Fe3+, Cu2+) were bought from Tianjin Chemical Reagent Company (Tianjin, China). tetracycline (TC, >99 %), DL-Cysteine (DL-Cys, >99 %), DL-histamine (DL-His, ≥99.5 %), l-glutamine (L-Glu, ≥98 %), tyrosine (Tyr, ≥99 %), phenylalanine (Phe, ≥98 %), glutathione (GSH, >98 %), ascorbic acid (VC, ≥99 %), thiamine (VB1, ≥99 %), riboflavin (VB2, ≥98 %), vitamin (VB6, ≥98 %), glucose (Glc, ≥98 %), sorbic acid (≥99 %), citric acid (≥99.8 %), mandelic acid (≥99 %) and erythromycin were procured by Sigma-Aldrich (St Louis, USA). 50 mM Britton-Robinson (BR) buffer solutions with different pH values were prepared by mixing suitable volumes of 50 mM H3PO4, H3BO3, CH3COOH and NaOH. During the whole research process, the water used was ultrapure water (Milli-Q) with resistivity of 18.2 MU.

The UV–vis absorption spectra were acquired on Shimadzu UV-2450 absorption spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). The Edinburgh Instrument FLS-920 spectrofluorometer (Livingston, UK) was used to measure and collect fluorescence spectra and fluorescence lifetimes. The infrared (IR) spectra of samples were measured by Nicolet Magna 550 infrared spectrometer (Thermo, Nicolet). The pH values of the solution were measured with a pH meter FE20 (Shanghai Mettler Instrument Company, Ltd., China). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) picture was presented by using the JEOL JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (Tokyo, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectrum (XPS) was captured with a Leybold Heraeus SKL-12 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Shanghai, China). The zeta potentials of the nanoparticles in the solution were recorded by Malvern Instruments Nano-ZS90 Zetasizer (Malvern, UK).

The glassware required for the experiment was completely cleaned with aqua regia (HNO3/HCl, 1:3) and ultrapure water. Freshly prepared AgNO3 (0.0025 M, 20 mL) and NAC (0.02 M, 5 mL) were completely mixed by stirring vigorously for 30 min. Subsequently, the mixed solution was poured into a 50 mL three-necked round bottom flask, and the reflux reaction apparatus were assembled in order. Then, set the reaction temperature to 80 °C, reflux time to 24 h and ensure the proper stirring speed in the reaction process. After standing and cooling to room temperature, Ag(0)@Ag(I)−NAC@Ag NCs (referred to as NAC@Ag NCs) emitting high bright green fluorescence were synthesized.

The quantum yield (QY) of the Ag NCs was obtained according to the following formula:
Φ x = Φ s × (As / Ax) × (Ix / Is) × (nx / ns)2
Here, “Φ” is the quantum yield, “A” is the absorbance, “I” refers to the fluorescence intensity integral area, and “n” is the refractive index of solvent. The subscript “x” and “s” refer to the testing sample (Ag NCs) and the control sample (quinine sulfate), respectively. The QY of quinine sulfate in 0.1 M sulfuric acid is 0.54 as the reference criterion.

A series of TC solutions with different concentrations (1.0 × 10−6 M, 1.0 × 10-5 M…, 1.0 M) were freshly prepared. 100 μL of NAC@Ag NCs solution was added to the mixed solution of 500 μL of BR (0.05 M, pH = 5) buffer solution and 500 μL of ethanol solution and the fluorescence spectrum was recorded. Then, different concentrations of TC solution were sequentially added to the silver nanocluster solution. After incubation for 2 min under the normal temperature, fluorescence spectra at different concentrations of TC were also recorded. The abscissa was the concentration of TC, and the ordinate was the corresponding fluorescence intensity value at this concentration. At last, a correlation linear curve was made to determine the concentration of TC.

In order to investigate the fluorescence selectivity toward TC, the following selective experiments were performed. TC (0.01 M, 20 μL) was added to NAC@Ag NCs to determine its fluorescence intensity as the blank control. 20 μL, 0.1 M common anions and cations (PO4 3–, CO3 2–, HCO3 –, F–, SO4 2–, Cl–, NO2 –, Ac–, SCN–, C2O4 2–, Ca2+, Mg2+, K+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Na+, Sn2+, Al3+, Fe3+, Cu2+) and small molecules (DL-Cys, DL-His, L-Glu, Tyr, Phe, GSH, VC, VB1, VB2, VB6, Glc, sorbic acid, citric acid, mandelic acid, erythromycin) of potential interfering substances were also added to NAC@Ag NCs respectively to measure the fluorescence intensity values after incubation for 2 min under the ambient temperature. Then the fluorescence intensity values were measured again after the addition of TC in NAC@Ag NCs solution in the presence of above potential interfering substances.

In order to evaluate the accuracy and feasibility of the NAC@Ag NCs sensing system for actual samples testing, the TC concentrations in actual milk samples were analyzed. Firstly, the glacial acetic acid (CH3COOH) was added into the milk samples to remove the protein before the experiment. Secondly, the milk samples were sonicated for 20 min to further precipitate the protein. The supernatant was added to the centrifuge tube and centrifuged for 10 min at 10,000 r/min. After centrifugal treatment, the lipid was removed by filtration through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane. And the supernatant of milk samples was diluted 30 times by using BR buffer solution (0.05 M, pH = 5) for analysis. The concentrations of TC in milk samples were measured by standard addition method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical procedure of TC detection in milk
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence analysis detection procedure: 100 μL of NAC@Ag NCs solution was added to the mixed solution of 500 μL of pre-treated milk solution and 500 μL of ethanol solution. Then the TC standard solution was added to the above solution to obtain the final solutions with different final concentrations of TC (4.50 μM, 9.00 μM, 26.5 μM). After incubation for 2 min under the normal temperature, fluorescence spectra were measured under an excitation wavelength of 400 nm. The measured fluorescence intensity was substituted into the linear equation to calculate the TC concentration. The measurement of each TC concentration was repeated 6 times and the RSD value was calculated. The TC recovery was calculated by dividing the measured TC content by the added TC content in the milk samples. In order to verify the accuracy and reliability of the fluorescence detection results, HPLC was used to measure the tetracycline content in milk samples at the same level. HPLC analysis detection conditions：column: Eclipse XDB-C18 column, 250 mm × 4.6 mm, 5 μm; mobile phase: 0.01 M oxalic acid/acetonitrile/methanol (80:15:5, v/v/v); flow rate: 1 mL/min; the column temperature: kept at 40 °C; injection volume: 20 μL; UV detection wavelength: fixed at 350 nm. A series of milk standard solutions containing 4.50 μM, 9.00 μM, and 26.5 μM tetracycline were prepared by standard addition method. The HPLC analysis results were compared with the fluorescence analysis results to evaluate the applicability of the analysis results.

The synthesis conditions including the molar ratio of NAC/AgNO3, reaction temperature and reflux time were optimized to obtain high-quality luminescent NAC@Ag NCs. Under the condition that the concentration of AgNO3 was constant, the different molar ratios of NAC/AgNO3 were designed to be 2:1, 3:1, 4:1, 5:1, 6:1, 7:1, 10:1. As portrayed in Fig. 1 A, the fluorescence spectra were recorded under different molar ratios. From these results it was concluded that the fluorescence intensity of 2:1 was higher than that of other molar ratios. Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity gradually decreased with the increase NAC/AgNO3 molar ratio. NAC@Ag NCs had almost no fluorescence when the ratio of NAC/AgNO3 was 10:1. Therefore, it was reasonable to choose 2:1 as the optimal molar ratio of NAC/AgNO3 to optimize other related parameters.

As the reaction temperature affected the reduction capacity of NAC, the fluorescence intensity of NAC@Ag NCs was also affected. The fluorescence intensity of NAC@Ag NCs synthesized under different reaction temperatures (50 °C, 60 °C, 70 °C, 80 °C, 90 °C) was investigated. As displayed in Fig. 1B, the results showed that the fluorescence intensity of 80 °C was higher than that of other temperatures. The formation process of highly luminescent NAC@Ag NCs was shown in Scheme 1, which was similar to that of aggregation-induced emission of Au(0)@Au(I)−thiolate core-shell nanoclusters synthesized by Luo et al. [55]. At the first step, AgNO3 and NAC were thoroughly mixed by vigorous stirring for 30 min at ambient temperature. NAC coordinated with Ag(I) via a thiol group (-SR) to form Ag(I)–SR complexes. At the second step, NAC selectively reduced part of Ag(I)–SR complexes or uncoordinated Ag(I) ions to Ag(0). Due to the high affinity between Ag(I) and Ag(0), Ag(0)-on-Ag(I)−SR intermediates were formed at the temperature of 80 °C. At the third step, Ag(0)-on-Ag(I)−SR intermediates slowly aggregated through Ag(0) atoms collision and fusion to form Ag(0) core and Ag(I)−SR complexes shell. The shell structure of Ag(0)@Ag(I)−NAC@Ag NCs synthesized in this way had a dense aggregates with high rate of Ag(I)−SR complexes. Very strong luminescence was emitted by the AIE of Ag NCs [55]. The results demonstrated that if the reaction temperature was lower than 60 °C, NAC was unable to effectively reduce Ag(I) to Ag(0). The necessary condition for the production of Ag(0)@Ag(I)–SR core-shell structure was the relatively high temperature [46]. It not only helped to break the bonding of some Ag(I)–SR complexes, but also reduced the uncoordinated Ag(I) ions to Ag(0), thus providing the precursor for the formation of Ag core [46]. If the temperature was higher than 80 °C, Ag(I)−SR complexes were reduced completely to Ag(0). So dense aggregates of large portions of Ag(I)−SR complexes may not form in the shell of the core-shell nanostructure, which further proved that strong luminescence was emitted by the AIE of Ag NCs. Therefore, 80 °C was the optimal reflux temperature in the synthesis process.

Fig. 1C depicted the fluorescence spectra of NAC@Ag NCs at different reflux time. As the reaction time increased from 12 h to 36 h, the fluorescence intensity of NAC@Ag NCs increased gradually, but the increasing trend was small. Considering the time factor, the reflux time was set as 24 h to synthesize NAC@Ag NCs.

The UV–vis absorption spectra (Fig. 2 A) revealed that the synthesized Ag NCs showed absorption peaks at 254, 288, and 397 nm respectively. The peak at 397 nm was considered as the surface plasmon resonance (SPR) absorption, which existed in Ag nanoparticles [56]. It suggested that the prepared Ag NCs had a larger diameter, which was caused by the aggregation of Ag(0)-on-Ag(I)−SR intermediates. The absorption peaks of AgNO3 and NAC were at 300 nm and 260 nm respectively also shown in Fig. 2A, which were very different from that of Ag NCs. It preliminary indicated that Ag NCs were successfully synthesized.

The fluorescence spectra of Ag NCs (Fig. 2A) showed that there was the optimal emission wavelength (λem) at 494 nm at a maximum excitation wavelength (λex) of 386 nm. The inset showed high bright green fluorescence at 365 nm ultraviolet radiation (Fig. 2A). Besides, excitation dependent luminescence studies were used to evaluate the quality of Ag NCs. The results showed that the emission wavelengths were stable at about 494 nm at different excitation wavelengths (Fig. 2B). This photoluminescence characteristic independent of the excitation wavelength implied the size of the synthesized nanoclusters was uniform [57]. As described in Fig. 2B, the Ag NCs still produced relatively strong fluorescence and the emission peak position remained unchanged under the excitation at 400 nm. In order to avoid the interference of the internal filtering effect in practical applications, 400 nm was selected as the excitation wavelength in subsequent sensing experiments.

In addition, long fluorescence lifetime was a characteristic of metal nanoclusters with aggregation-induced emission property. Fig. 2C showed the fluorescence lifetime spectrum of Ag NCs. There were two lifetimes of τ1 2.62 μs (18.48 %) and τ2 12.99 μs (81.52 %) with an average lifetime of 11.07 μs. The long lifetime meant that the Ag NCs had high emission efficiency. The findings implied that radiative decay was associated with the cross-over between systems and then probably lead to phosphorescence [58]. The QY of the Ag NCs was estimated to be 0.44 with quinine sulfate as the control, which was higher than the QY of most high-luminescence metal nanoclusters reported previously (typically 0.05−0.25) [31,46,47,58]. We speculated that the high bright luminescence behavior of NAC@Ag NCs was caused by transferring of charge from ligand (NAC) to Ag(0) ((LMCT)) and especially ligand (NAC) to Ag(I) to Ag(0) (LMMCT) that attributed to the dense aggregates of Ag(I)−SR complexes in the shell of Ag NCs. The aggregation of Ag NCs inhibited the intramolecular vibration and rotation of the ligand NAC. Thus the excited radiationless relaxation behavior associated with the ligand was inhibited, and the luminescence performance was significantly enhanced [39,55]. And the aggregation of Ag NCs allowed charge transfer to be carried out between different clusters, and further enhanced their luminescence behavior.

The size distribution of NAC@Ag NCs was analysed by TEM. As described in Fig. 2D, the prepared high bright Ag NCs exhibited an aggregate state. The NCs aggregates was composed of smaller Ag NCs which had an average particle diameter of 1.39 nm (Fig. 2E and statistical analysis according to the inset of Fig. 2D), indicating that the strong luminescent property of Ag NCs was caused by the mechanism of AIE.

Infrared spectrometry was used to estimate the bonding of NAC on the surface of Ag NCs. Fig. 2F depicted the infrared spectra of ligand NAC and NAC@Ag NCs. It was not hard to see that the infrared spectra of NAC and NAC@Ag NCs were very similar. The absorption band at 3375 cm−1 was considered to be the stretching vibration of -OH and -NH [59]. The amide I band ν C=O (the stretching vibration of CO) of NAC and Ag NCs appeared at 1717 cm−1 and 1734 cm−1 respectively, and the amide II band δNH (the bending vibration of NH) appeared at 1534 cm−1 and 1537 cm−1 respectively. The slight shift in the amide I band and the amide II band might have connection with the bonding mode of the functional groups between NAC and Ag NCs. Moreover, the infrared spectrum of NAC showed a strong absorption band (-SH) at 2547 cm−1, but Ag NCs had no characteristic absorption in this infrared region. The results indicated that NAC formed Ag–S bond by combining with Ag atom and successfully wrapped on the surface of Ag NCs.

Another characteristic of AIE type metal NCs was the high oxidation state of metal species (M), which was mainly owing to the presence of a considerable amount of M(I)–thiolate complexes in the synthesized nanoclusters [46]. The elemental composition of Ag NCs and the chemical valence state of Ag were obtained by XPS. Fig. 3 A showed a full spectrum of elements of the synthesized Ag NCs, illustrating that Ag NCs were comprised of Ag, C, N, O, S elements. Fig. 3B demonstrated the XPS spectra of the Ag 3d peak of synthesized Ag NCs at 367.7 eV and 373.7 eV, which were referred to the binding energies of Ag 3d5/2 and Ag 3d3/2 respectively. The peak at 367.7 eV was between Ag(I) (367.5 eV) and pure Ag(0) (368.2 eV), confirming the presence of both Ag(Ⅰ) and Ag(0) in the Ag NCs [60]. Furthermore, The Ag 3d5/2 XPS spectrum of Ag NCs was divided into Ag(I) of 367.5 eV and Ag(0) of 368.2 eV [61,62]. The ratio of Ag(I) to Ag(0) content in the NAC@Ag NCs was calculated as 2.25/1, which implied that there was a high percentage of Ag(I)−SR complexes in the cluster.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH and the stability of Ag NCs
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the changes in the solubility of surface ligands under different pH, the aggregation of metal nanoclusters was controlled by the solution pH values. The aggregation-induced emission phenomenon of Ag NCs by changing the pH of the system had been verified [47]. As described in Fig. 4 A, under acidic conditions, the fluorescence intensity of the Ag NCs decreased continuously as the pH value increased. When the pH value is equal to or more than 7.5, there was almost no emission obtained. It was also found that the aggregation of nanoclusters was sensitive to pH in other AIE type metal NCs [58]. The zeta potentials of Ag NCs under pH 3–6 were explored. As displayed in Fig. S1, the zeta potential of Ag NCs was measured as 0 mV approximately under pH 3.0. They were -12.1, -18.4 and -29.1 mV when the solution pH values were 4.0, 5.0 and 6.0 respectively. The ligand NAC contained carboxylic acid group (−COOH) which was deprotonated with a higher charge in the high pH solution. The negatively charged Ag(I)−NAC complexes on the Ag(0) core made Ag NCs electrostatic repelling from one another. With the increase of pH, the aggregation of Ag NCs weakened gradually. Consequently, the as-synthesized Ag NCs had no almost AIE behavior at pH higher than 7.5.

High salt effect has long been known to enhance the total concentration of ions in the solution and the interaction between the ions, which was applied to screen the charge repulsion of the highly luminescent Ag NCs. As depicted in Fig. 4B, the fluorescence intensity of Ag NCs decreased along with the increase of the concentration of NaCl. Due to the increase of the concentration of ions in the solution and the enhancement of the interaction between the ions, the repulsion of the negatively charged Ag NCs was enhanced. So that the aggregation behavior of the nanoclusters was reduced, which gradually weakened the fluorescence intensity. Similarly, the fluorescence intensity of Ag NCs also decreased with the increase of concentration of BR buffer due to the effect of salt effect (Fig. 4C), which further proved that the ionic strength had a significant effect on the AIE behavior of the nanoclusters.

As displayed in Fig. 4D, the fluorescence intensity of NAC@Ag NCs in BR buffer solution decreased 10 % under continuous UV radiation for 35 min (curve b). However, the NAC@Ag NCs in the mixture of BR buffer solution and ethanol (v:v = 1:1) had a good photostability (curve a), since the fluorescence intensity remained unchange when continuous UV radiation was operated. The effect of the amount of ethanol on the fluorescence intensity of NAC@Ag NCs was investigated, and the experimental design was shown in Table S2. The results illustrated that the amount of ethanol did not cause much effect on the fluorescence intensity of the Ag NCs as shown in Fig. 4E. Although the slight decrease in polarity of the solvent did not affect the fluorescence intensity, the aggregated Ag NCs were more stable with the addition of ethanol, in which the diffusion of the clusters was inhibited. The solvent ethanol (50 % by volume) was used for the application and storage of the Ag NCs. As shown in Fig. 4F, the fluorescence intensity of Ag NCs did not change when stored in 4 °C for six months. The inset photographs showed that the Ag NCs still emit bright green fluorescence after six months. Fig. S2 depicted the XPS spectra of the Ag 3d peak of Ag NCs after six months of storage. The ratio of Ag(I) to Ag(0) content in the NAC@Ag NCs was calculated as 2.30/1, which is essentially the same as the ratio of freshly synthesized Ag NCs (2.25/1). It indicated that the synthesized NAC@Ag NCs had good antioxidant properties. In summary, the synthesized Ag NCs had good stability and was used for optical and chemical sensing field.

Based on the fact that the fluorescence intensity of NAC@Ag NCs decreased or even quenched with the addition of tetracycline (TC), a sensing system for detecting TC had been constructed. Fig. 5 A examined the fluorescence intensity of 20−200 μL Ag NCs in the absence and presence of TC (F0 and F). As the amount of Ag NCs increased from 10 μL to 160 μL, the fluorescence intensity (F0) also gradually increased. However, when the amount of Ag NCs was higher than 160 μL, the rising trend became relatively slow probably, which might owing to the self-absorption of Ag NCs. The fluorescence intensity (F) was decreased when TC existed the Ag NCs system. By calculating the decrease value of fluorescence intensity F0–F, it was easy to see from the trend in the Fig. 5A that the amount of 100 μL of Ag NCs was the optimal amount for the detection of TC.

The response time was one of the most important parameters of fluorescence sensing detection methods. A standard TC solution with a known concentration was added to Ag NCs, when a stable fluorescence signal was obtained, this time was defined as the response time [63]. The fluorescence intensity decreased within 2 min and remained stable for the next 30 min when TC was added to Ag NCs (Fig. 5B), which suggested the reaction system reached equilibrium rapidly. Therefore, the optimal response time for TC detection was 2 min. It also showed that Ag NCs had a fast response to TC and achieved rapid detection of TC.

The sensing experiments between Ag NCs and TC were performed under optimal test conditions. Fig. 5C depicted the fluorescence spectra of Ag NCs with different TC concentrations. With the gradual increase of TC concentration, the fluorescence intensity of Ag NCs decreased gradually. And the purpose of thoroughly quenching the fluorescence of Ag NCs was achieved when the TC content was 230 μM. Meanwhile, the maximum emission band remained at about 494 nm without significant deviation. As depicted in Fig. 5D, when TC concentration was between 1.12 and 230 μM, the relative fluorescence intensity showed a fine linear relationship, which was expressed as log (F0/F) = 0.004 [TC] (μM) + 0.011 (R2 = 0.997). Here, F0 was the fluorescence intensity of Ag NCs in the absence of TC, F was the fluorescence intensity in the presence of TC, and [TC] was the concentration of TC. Furthermore, the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated as 0.47 μM according to the formula LOD = 3σ/k, where σ was the standard deviation of fluorescence intensity of Ag NCs (n = 11) and the k value was the slope in the linear graph as shown in Fig. 5D.

In addition, the detection approach proposed in this study was compared with other existing TC detection methods based on fluorescence nanomaterials (Table 1 ) [64–70]. Compared with other methods, the synthesis of NAC@Ag NCs only needed one step. Moreover, the synthesis of NAC@Ag NCs was environmentally friendly because there was no addition of organic solvent and strong base compound during the reaction. NAC@Ag NCs had excellent luminescent properties, high QY of 0.44 and microsecond long life of 11.07 μs. Remarkably, the incubation time in determination of tetracycline needed only two minutes, indicating that the proposed sensing method had the potential of rapid detection of tetracycline. Besides, the Ag NCs probe in this study showed a wider range of linearity and relatively lower detection limit than other methods.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of Ag NCs toward TC
ce:section.ce:para: These effects of potential interfering substances existed in actual samples on the fluorescence sensing method for TC was explored. Parallel competition experiments of various coexisting small molecules and ionic species were studied under the same conditions, including DL-Cys, DL-His, L-Glu, Tyr, Phe, GSH, VC, VB1, VB2, VB6, Glc, sorbic acid, citric acid, mandelic acid, erythromycin, PO4 3–, CO3 2–, HCO3 –, F–, SO4 2–, Cl–, NO2 –, Ac–, SCN–, C2O4 2–, Ca2+, Mg2+, K+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Na+, Sn2+, Al3+, Fe3+ and Cu2+. Fig. 6 A and 6B depicted the relative fluorescence intensity (F0-F)/F0 of Ag NCs with various coexisting small molecules and ions. It was discovered that the relative fluorescence intensity would not change substantially after the addition of other small molecules and ions. Fig. 6C also showed the photographs of the Ag NCs solutions after the addition of TC and various potential interferences under 365 nm UV irradiation. It was clear to see that the system only in the condition for the existence of TC was able to activate the fluorescence quenching of Ag NCs immediately and significantly, even the concentrations of potential coexisting small molecules and ionic species were 10-fold higher than that of TC. In brief, the NAC@Ag NCs probe had the merit of good selectivity for TC, which had certain application value for the selective analysis of TC in practical samples.

The detection mechanism for TC by Ag NCs sensing system was discussed. Fig. 7 A displayed the excitation spectrum of Ag NCs and the UV–vis absorption spectroscopy of TC. Apparently, the TC showed strong absorption bands at 275 nm and 356 nm, which overlapped the Ag NCs excitation spectrum very well. So, it indicated that the mechanism of fluorescence quenching for TC might be caused by fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) or inner filter effect (IFE) [71]. The type of fluorescence sensing mechanism was further determined by measuring the fluorescence lifetime (Fig. S3). The fluorescence lifetime of Ag NCs kept constant in the presence of TC ([TC] = 135 μM), which suggested that the quenching mechanism was static quenching instead of dynamic quenching. It also evidenced that the fluorescence quenching was the IFE mechanism rather than FRET mechanism, because FRET had the characteristic of the significant change in fluorescence lifetime [72]. Fluorescence internal filtration effect generally referred to that the excitation or emission spectra of the fluorophores were absorbed by the light-absorbing substances, so as to reduce or even quench the fluorescence intensity [73,74]. It illustrated that TC absorbs the fluorescence at the excitation spectrum of Ag NCs, thereby the fluorescence was quenched in this study.

In addition, the infrared spectra of Ag NCs without TC and with TC were measured respectively, and the measured infrared spectra were compared (Fig. 7B). After the addition of TC in Ag NCs solution, the stretching vibration absorption bands of −OH and -NH- were weakened at 3375 cm−1, while the new absorption band appeared at 3426 cm−1. In addition, the CO stretch vibration band was red-shifted to 1737 cm−1. These results indicated that −OH in TC formed hydrogen bonds with -NH- and −OH on the surface of nanoclusters, which made Ag NCs target TC with high selectivity and sensitivity. The UV absorption spectrum of Ag NCs changed significantly after the addition of TC (Fig. 7C). The UV absorption peak at 397 nm disappeared and the absorption peak at 288 nm blue shifted. Moreover, a new absorption peak appeared at 390 nm, indicating that a coordination compound in the ground state formed between Ag NCs and TC. With the addition of TC, the zeta potential of Ag NCs changed from -18.4 mV to -6.62 mV (Fig.7D and S4). It was possible that −COO- group on the surface of Ag NCs were consumed due to the addition of TC. TC interacted with the surface groups of Ag NCs and formed new bond. These results further indicated that the interaction between TC and Ag NCs occurred in the ground state, and the fluorescence quenching type was static quenching. In conclusion, the above results demonstrated that the mechanism of TC detection was IFE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of TC in milk samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to explore the potential value of NAC@Ag NCs detection method for TC in practical samples, TC levels in milk samples were measured by standard addition methods. The TC concentrations in milk samples were calculated by the linear equation log (F0/F) = 0.004 [TC] (μM) + 0.011 (Fig. 5D). The analytical results were displayed in Table 2 , and the recovery values were in the range of 98.2%–102% and the RSD lower than 1.9 %. To further prove the accuracy and reliability of this method, the high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) with Eclipse XDB-C18 column was also used to measure TC concentrations in milk samples. The detection results were shown in Table 2. The concentrations of TC measured by the two methods were approximately the same, which further illustrated the proposed NAC@Ag NCs-based fluorescence sensing method was an accurate and reliable method for detecting TC concentration.64–70

All in all, the aggregation-induced emission NAC@Ag NCs with core-shell structure were synthesized successfully by reflux method using AgNO3 as raw material and NAC as ligand. The optimum conditions of the synthesis were that the optimal molar ratio of NAC/AgNO3 was 2:1, the reflux temperature was 80 °C and the reflux time was 24 h. The synthesis procedure was simple and pollution-free, avoiding the addition of common reductants such as sodium borohydride, ascorbic acid and surfactants. The obtained Ag NCs had good aggregation-induced emission property and emitted strong green fluorescence with an optimal emission wavelength of 494 nm, high fluorescence QY of 0.44, long microsecond lifetime of 11.07 μs and high content of Ag(I) component. More interestingly, the fluorescence of Ag NCs was quenched by TC due to the mechanism of the IFE and the static quenching effect. The obtained Ag NCs were able to detect the TC selectivity and sensitivity with the range of linearity from 1.12–230 μM and a detection limit of 0.47 μM. The synthesized Ag NCs probe had good sensitivity and selectivity, wide range of linearity, low detection limit for TC and was used for the determination of the content of TC in actual samples.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yan Zhang earned her BSc in chemistry and PhD degree in analytical chemistry in 2004 and 2009 from Shanxi University, respectively. She is now working as a professor in Shanxi University and her current research interests include the nanomaterial and chemical sensing analysis.
Mei Lv obtained BSc degree in Chemistry from Yuncheng University in 2018. Now she is a postgraduate in Shanxi University and major in analytical chemistry.

Pengfei Gao earned his BSc degree in chemistry, MSc degree in organic chemistry and PhD degree in applied chemistry from Shanxi University in 2004, 2007 and 2011, respectively. He is now working as an associate professor in Shanxi University and his current research interests include nanomaterials.

Guomei Zhang received her BSc degree from the Shanxi University of China (Taiyuan) in 1997, her MSc degree and PhD degree in analytical and inorganic chemistry in 2003 and 2006 from Shanxi University, respectively. She joined the Chemistry Faculty of Shanxi University in 2006, and master tutor at Shanxi University since 2008 and has been a professor in 2014. Her current research interests include the DNA assembly and photoluminescence analysis of nanomaterials.

Lihong Shi is now working as a professor in Shanxi University, Shanxi Province, China. She obtained her PhD degree in physical chemistry from Shanxi Institute of Coal Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences in 2008. Her current research interests include nanomaterial and chemosensors.

Mingjian Yuan obtained PhD degree in the Institute of Chemistry Chinese Academy of Sciences in 2009. He also did post-doctoral research in the Washington University (America) and Toronto University (Canada) from 2009 to 2016. He is now a distinguished researcher in the Nankai University. His current research interests include colloidal quantum dots luminescent materials, photoelectric conversion materials and photoelectric conversion devices.

Shaomin Shuang received her BSc degree in chemistry and MSc degree in analytical chemistry in 1986 and 1989 from Shanxi University, respectively, and PhD degree in 1998 from South China University of Technology. She has been a professor at Shanxi University since 2002. Her current research interests include analytical chemistry and development of biosensors and chemosensors.
